metric_name: Visual Basic and PowerBuilder market dominance
metric_value: Most popular first-gen CADEs are Microsoft Visual Basic and Sybase/Powersoft PowerBuilder; major users in many cases contemplating new toolsets

metric_name: Data warehouse growth rate
metric_value: 6-7 times growth in database size over 18 months in many data warehouse cases; requires next-gen CADE scalability

metric_name: Visual Basic repository limitation
metric_value: TI-Microsoft initiative shows no signs of finishing; only fruit is TI's administrator-intensive departmental Arranger; Microsoft shows no intention of targeting fully-featured high-end development

metric_name: PowerBuilder repository delivery timeline uncertainty
metric_value: Sybase/Powersoft committed to using repository from recently acquired company; delivery date not yet clear; use for next-gen technology even further off

metric_name: Centura repository capabilities
metric_value: Full-featured object-based repository; supports Oracle and Sybase data-dictionary metadata import; Team Object Manager for repository administration

metric_name: Centura Application Server status
metric_value: Beta testing in 2Q96; provides drag-and-drop application partitioning across 2-tier and 3-tier architectures; supports DCE, TCP/IP, Network OLE communications

metric_name: Centura Internet capabilities
metric_value: Internet QuickObjects for Web-enabled SQL data access; Centura Web Data Server replicates data from multiple databases with firewall security; Java compilation planned for subsequent release

metric_name: First-gen CADE problem: Process and culture scalability
metric_value: First-gen CADEs provide little software for scaling development process or automating unfamiliar tasks for training; communication problems increase as square of programmer count

metric_name: First-gen CADE problem: Application scalability
metric_value: First-gen CADEs provide little application partitioning, query optimization, or load balancing; applications slow drastically as users/database size increase

metric_name: First-gen CADE problem: Deployment and maintenance costs
metric_value: Absence of automated deployment/maintenance software requires physical installation at each site, local admin burden, repeated redeployment on upgrades

metric_name: First-gen CADE problem: Architectural inflexibility
metric_value: First-gen CADEs lack repositories and application-repartitioning GUIs for adapting to 3-tier and Intranet architectures

metric_name: First-gen CADE problem: Programmer productivity ceiling
metric_value: Complex/data-intensive applications require reversion to 4GL or 3GL scripting; most productivity gains lost for large applications

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Team programming tools
metric_value: Version control, project management, regression testing; Centura: Team Object Manager + Team Object Repository + integrated PVCS version control

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Application partitioning
metric_value: Client-server partition via drag-and-drop VPE; Centura Application Server targeted at 2-tier and 3-tier partitioning

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Load balancing via TP monitor
metric_value: Centura supports Novell Tuxedo TP monitor with Tuxedo Deployment Suite; middleware access to IBM CICS and Encina; 3-Tier Wizard for simplified TP programming

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Query optimization and distributed DB
metric_value: Centura SAL provides extensive database/gateway operations; Centura Ranger for heterogeneous replication across multiple vendors' databases

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Full lifecycle support
metric_value: Design (Rational Rose/LogicWorks ERWin links), programming/testing (debugger, incremental compiler), deployment (repository-based), maintenance (SQLConsole integration)

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Repository
metric_value: Full-featured object-oriented repository; Team Object Repository on standard RDBMSs; open to Oracle and Sybase metadata; key differentiator vs. Visual Basic and PowerBuilder

metric_name: Next-gen CADE criterion: Business modeling
metric_value: Centura Data Model Viewer for business modeling; reverse engineering of schemas from modeler, CASE tools, or RDBMS metadata

metric_name: OOP development speed improvement (fragmentary studies)
metric_value: Average start-to-ship development speed improvement ~35% after 1+ year learning curve, per fragmentary studies cited by Aberdeen
notes: Aberdeen cites 'fragmentary studies'; low confidence due to limited evidence cited.

metric_name: CADE market taxonomy: First-generation and departmental
metric_value: Visual Basic, PowerBuilder, Delphi, SQLWindows; best for departmental/simple/client-centric apps not needing scale

metric_name: CADE market taxonomy: Next-gen CADEs scaling first-gen
metric_value: Gupta Centura, Symantec, Unify, Compuware Uniface; appropriate for scaling up existing first-gen applications and new moderate-scale development
notes: CADE category 2 of 4 — Aberdeen's recommended category for most users.

metric_name: CADE market taxonomy: High-end next-gen from RDBMS suppliers
metric_value: Oracle, Sybase, Informix, CA-Ingres, Progress CADEs; useful for installed RDBMS base for upgrading from 4GL and data-intensive applications

metric_name: CADE market taxonomy: Ultra-high-end next-gen
metric_value: Forte, Dynasty, Seer Technologies; best for very complex and very data-intensive new applications with multiple suppliers' databases

metric_name: Prediction: Visual Basic and PowerBuilder users face serious scalability constraints
metric_value: In short to medium term, users may have serious concerns about VB/PowerBuilder shops' ability to solve first-gen problems without bringing in new toolset

metric_name: Prediction: Gupta Centura as leading next-gen CADE for migration
metric_value: Aberdeen recommends Gupta Centura on short lists; builds on full-featured repository to deliver next-gen technologies; especially appropriate for scaling existing apps

metric_name: Outcome: Gupta Technologies / Centura fate
metric_value: Gupta renamed to Centura Software (1995); passed through investors (Warp Technology Holdings 2005, Unify Corporation 2006, renamed back to Gupta Technologies 2012); acquired by OpenText 2015; Centura did not become dominant next-gen CADE
notes: Source: Wikipedia/Gupta Technologies (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gupta_Technologies). Aberdeen's prediction of Centura dominance did not materialize; Java EE and .NET emerged as the dominant platforms.

metric_name: Outcome: Visual Basic/PowerBuilder scalability constraints realized
metric_value: PowerBuilder adopted by Sybase but never achieved full-featured repository; Visual Basic evolved to VB.NET (2002) with significant breaking changes; both tools lost significant market share to Java and C# as Aberdeen predicted
notes: Source: PowerBuilder Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PowerBuilder). Aberdeen correctly identified the scalability problem but misjudged the solution (Centura vs. Java/C#).

metric_name: Outcome: Java and .NET replaced the CADE category
metric_value: Java EE (1999) and Microsoft .NET (2002) provided enterprise-scale application development frameworks that made the CADE category largely obsolete; Aberdeen's CADE evaluation framework was correct but the winner was not Centura
notes: The scalability problems Aberdeen identified were real; the solution was platform-level (Java/CLR) rather than CADE-level.

metric_name: Outcome: 3-tier architecture became standard enterprise pattern
metric_value: 3-tier architecture became the standard enterprise application pattern throughout the late 1990s and 2000s, exactly as Aberdeen predicted; web tier, application server, database tier became ubiquitous
notes: Aberdeen's architectural prediction was entirely correct; only the specific tooling prediction (Centura) missed.
